one_line_takeaway: QLPro uses LLM triple-voting to classify CodeQL taint specs and a Writer/Executor/Repairer loop to auto-generate compilable CodeQL rules, lifting Java vulnerability detection from CodeQL's 24/62 to 41/62 and finding 2 confirmed 0-days.
key_finding: The best configuration, two collaborating Claude-3.7-Sonnet-thinking instances, produced QL files with 90.90% syntactic (compilation) correctness and detected 41 of the 62 known vulnerabilities (66.10%), versus only 24/62 (38.70%) for the official CodeQL rule repository. It also surfaced 6 previously unknown vulnerabilities, 2 of which developers officially confirmed as 0-days (the other 4 pending), demonstrating detection beyond the stock ruleset.
core_contribution: QLPro is a framework that couples LLMs with the CodeQL static analysis tool to fully automate the generation of project-specific vulnerability scanning (QL) rules, so that even non-security developers can audit entire open-source Java projects without hand-writing rules. Its two novel components are (1) a triple-voting mechanism that classifies CodeQL-extracted APIs into source/sink/none by evaluating each API in three separate LLM contexts and taking a majority vote (to counter context-interference bias when APIs are batched), and (2) a three-role mechanism (Writer R_w, Executor R_e, Repairer R_r) in which the Writer drafts a QL file from a (source, sink) tuple, the Executor tries to compile it, and on failure the Repairer analyzes the compiler error and feeds fixes back to the Writer, iterating up to a MAX limit. Note: the Extraction step (pulling all APIs/taint specifications from a project) is done by CodeQL itself, not a QLPro invention; QLPro's own contributions are the voting-based classification/pairing and the role-based rule synthesis on top of that extraction.
threat_model: Defender-side (blue-team) vulnerability discovery. The auditor has full access to a project's source code and wants to find latent vulnerabilities (SQL injection, arbitrary file upload/read, path traversal, etc.) before an attacker exploits them. The implicit adversary is an external attacker who controls tainted inputs (sources) that reach dangerous operations (sinks); QLPro's job is to surface those source-to-sink taint chains automatically. The paper does not frame LLM-specific or adversarial-ML threats; the LLM is the analysis tool, not the attack surface.
limitations: (1) Scope is Java-only and requires projects to be fully CodeQL-compilable, limiting generality. (2) Small benchmark: 10 projects / 62 vulnerabilities, so detection percentages rest on a handful of cases. (3) 'Correctness' means compilation success only - a syntactic proxy that says nothing about semantic soundness or whether a rule actually captures the intended taint path. (4) The paper reports vulnerabilities detected but provides no false-positive rate, precision, or manual-triage cost, so the practical noise burden of the generated rules is unknown. (5) Of the 6 claimed 0-days only 2 are confirmed. (6) No clean ablation isolating the individual gain of triple-voting vs the three-role mechanism; improvements are reported only at the whole-system level. (7) Reliance on closed-source Claude/GPT models makes cost and reproducibility non-trivial, and the MAX iteration cap's effect is not characterized.

datasets_used: JavaTest (newly constructed): 10 popular open-source Java projects from GitHub, 62 confirmed vulnerabilities, averaging 10.53K GitHub stars and ~50K LOC per project (largest >100K LOC)
1924 (source, sink) pairs / 1924 QL files generated from JavaTest for the syntactic-correctness evaluation
quantitative_results: Syntactic correctness (RQ1): Claude-3.7 70.01% (1347 files), Claude-3.7-thinking 75.47% (1452), two Claude-3.7-thinking 90.90% (1749), Claude-3.7 + GPT-4o-mini 79.10% (1522)
Known-vuln detection (RQ2, out of 62): Claude-3.7 29 (46.80%), Claude-3.7-thinking 29 (46.80%), two Claude-3.7-thinking 41 (66.10%), Claude-3.7 + GPT-4o-mini 31 (50.00%), official CodeQL 24 (38.70%)

0-day discovery (RQ3): 6 previously unknown vulnerabilities found by QLPro (two Claude-3.7-thinking config); 2 officially confirmed as 0-days, 4 pending; types include SQL injection, arbitrary file upload, arbitrary file read; example 0-day = arbitrary file upload via directory-traversal filename (e.g. ../../1.jsp) inside an uploaded archive
None of the 6 unknown vulnerabilities were detected by the official CodeQL rule set
baselines_compared: Official CodeQL rule repository (state-of-the-art static analysis baseline): 24/62 detected
Single-model QLPro ablations: Claude-3.7, Claude-3.7-thinking, and Claude-3.7 + GPT-4o-mini configurations
